one_line_takeaway: A survey-of-surveys synthesis (60 primary studies) proposing a unified factuality/faithfulness/compatibility taxonomy of code-LLM hallucination causes, mitigation strategies, and benchmarks -- a reliability/software-engineering contribution, not a security study, with security topics (supply-chain package hallucination, malicious LLM misuse) appearing only as third-party cited findings.
key_finding: Publication-trend analysis (the paper's own bibliometric result, Fig. 6-7): code-hallucination publications rose from 5 (2023) to 20 (2024), with 13 in the first half of 2025; arXiv preprints account for 54% of code-hallucination venues (vs. 30% conference, 16% journal); by contribution type, 58% propose new methods, 27% are empirical studies, 14% introduce benchmarks, and only 1% (a single paper) is itself a survey -- the gap the authors use to motivate this SLR. NLP-hallucination survey output peaked at 12 papers in 2024. Substantively, the review's synthesized taxonomy (Table 3) shows factuality and compatibility hallucinations are closely related (both harm code correctness) but distinct in origin: factuality errors are intrinsic to the code (e.g., API/library misuse), while compatibility errors are logically correct code that fails due to environment/dependency mismatch. The review also formalizes, via cited prior work, that code and natural language differ fundamentally in formal-language terms (Table 4): programming languages are largely context-free and pushdown-automaton-tractable with low ambiguity and low error tolerance, whereas natural language requires Turing-machine-level expressiveness and tolerates ambiguity -- offered as the structural explanation for why NLP hallucination-mitigation techniques (RAG, CoT, model editing, agent collaboration, post-editing) require code-specific adaptation (e.g., project-specific API resolution, dependency-aware constrained decoding, execution/test-based feedback) rather than direct transfer.
core_contribution: A systematic literature review (SLR), following the Kitchenham/Petersen SLR guidelines, of 60 studies (retrieved from ACM Digital Library, IEEE Xplore, and Web of Science, searched on 2025-05-25, out of 113 candidate studies after screening) on hallucination in code-oriented LLMs. The paper's own contribution is synthesis, not new methods or experiments: (1) a unified taxonomy of code hallucination organized around three dimensions -- factuality (Knowledge Hallucinations: API misuse, library misuse, language-feature misuse), faithfulness (Functional Misalignment: functional/non-functional requirement violation, logic-flow violation), and compatibility (Environment and Dependency Hallucinations: environment conflicts, dependency hallucination, resource-path errors); (2) a taxonomy of hallucination causes across model-level (decoding drift, exposure bias, knowledge gaps, spurious generalization), prompt/input, training-data, and project/environment factors; (3) a taxonomy of five mitigation-method families (model tuning/constrained decoding/attention intervention, model editing, prompt/reasoning strategies, knowledge-augmented generation, agent-based interaction, post-editing) mapped onto both general-NLP and code-specific literature; (4) a structured comparison of programming languages vs. natural languages via automata theory (PL as context-free/pushdown-automaton-tractable vs. NL requiring Turing-machine-level context); (5) a catalog of benchmarks and formalized hallucination/quality metrics (MiHN, MaHR, pass@k, TR-N, TR-S, Jaccard/edit similarity, BuildSuccessRate, Hallucination Rate); and (6) an enumerated set of open challenges (hallucination vs. mistake, confidence vs. hallucination, generalization-hallucination trade-off, absence of design-pattern awareness, synthetic-data factuality, private/sensitive-data exposure) and opportunities (unified evaluation benchmarks, explainable hallucination detection).
threat_model: n/a -- this is a reliability-focused systematic literature review with no adversarial threat model of its own. It does not propose an attacker, defender, or attack surface; it catalogs hallucination phenomena (unintentional model failures) and mitigation techniques as reported across 60 surveyed primary studies. Security-adjacent topics that appear (e.g., package/dependency hallucination as a software-supply-chain risk, malicious use of LLMs for phishing/social engineering, LLM-generated code security via CODEGUARD+) are attributed entirely to cited third-party studies (Spracklen et al., Krishna et al., Majumdar et al., Fu et al.) discussed as part of the literature synthesis, not framed or extended as the survey's own security analysis.
limitations: Stated by the paper itself in the Challenges section (Section 8, its own analysis rather than surveyed findings): (1) hallucination is difficult to distinguish operationally from an ordinary mistake, since both can manifest as incorrect output and only hallucination involves confident-but-unfounded generation; (2) high model confidence/low perplexity does not indicate factual correctness ("confident hallucination"), so confidence-based signals are insufficient for detection; (3) an unresolved trade-off exists between generalization (desirable) and hallucination (undesirable), since models with low generalization error can still hallucinate significantly; (4) code LLMs lack explicit design-pattern awareness, producing syntactically valid code that violates architectural intent in ways harder to detect than syntax errors; (5) synthetic training data used to address data scarcity/privacy can itself contain hallucinated or biased content that propagates into trained models; (6) hallucination can expose private or sensitive data (internal project identifiers, proprietary code) memorized from training corpora, a risk the authors flag as "critical yet underexplored." Additional limitations evident from the methodology itself and not fully elaborated by the authors in the extracted pages: the search covered only three databases as of a single cutoff (2025-05-25), producing a modest final corpus (60 studies) supplemented by manual snowballing; the pilot inter-rater agreement (Cohen's Kappa 0.59) was only "moderate," indicating some subjectivity in inclusion/exclusion judgments even after criteria refinement; and existing benchmarks reviewed under RQ4 are concentrated in Python and Java, limiting cross-language generalizability of any conclusions drawn from them.

quantitative_results: Paper's own SLR corpus stats (Table 1): code-hallucination search yielded 67 hits before dedup, 61 after dedup, 36 retained after full review; NLP-hallucination-survey search yielded 46 hits before and after dedup, 24 retained after review; final combined corpus = 60 studies from 113 title/abstract candidates.
Paper's own inter-rater reliability: Cohen's Kappa = 0.59 (moderate) on 30-paper pilot, 0.73 (substantial) on full assessment.

Paper's own publication-trend bibliometrics (Fig. 6-7): code-hallucination publications = 5 (2023), 20 (2024), 13 (H1 2025); venue mix = arXiv 54%, conference 30%, journal 16%; contribution-type mix = new methods 58%, empirical study 27%, benchmark 14%, survey 1%; NLP-hallucination survey output peaked at 12 papers in 2024.
Reported by Mundler et al. (surveyed work, not this paper's result): self-contradiction found in 17.7% of all sentences produced by ChatGPT.
Reported by Dziri et al. (surveyed work): more than 60% of benchmark responses in a knowledge-grounded conversational-model study contained hallucinations.
Reported by Arrieta et al. (surveyed work): DeepSeek-R1 (70B) produced unsafe responses in 12% of cases vs. 1.2% for OpenAI o3-mini in a safety assessment.
Reported by Tian et al. (surveyed benchmark CodeHaluEval): 8,883 samples across 699 tasks.
Reported by Spracklen et al. (surveyed work): package-hallucination study covering 576k samples across 16 LLMs.
Reported by Jiang et al. (surveyed benchmark Collu-Bench): over 13,000 instances for code hallucination in generation and program repair.
Reported by Huang et al. (surveyed benchmark HalluEditBench): over 6,000 hallucinations across 9 domains, evaluated across 5 dimensions (efficacy, generalization, portability, locality, robustness).
Reported by Zhuo et al. (surveyed work): manual annotation of 3,892 method-level and 2,560 parameter-level API misuses in LLM-generated code.
Reported by Tanzil et al. (surveyed tool CID): F1-score of 0.74-0.75 detecting incorrect ChatGPT responses in library-selection tasks.
Reported by Yu et al. (surveyed benchmark CoderEval): 230 Python and 230 Java code-generation problems from real-world projects.
